macro_architecture.reasoning: Map-Reduce analysis of 4 containers with 737 total components

layer_structure: Data Access / Integration (Adapters, Interceptors, API Modules)

container_analyses.summary: The backend follows a layered architecture (Presentation, Service, Repository, Domain) built on Spring Boot and related Spring projects, exposing its functionality through REST APIs. It implements a comprehensive deed registry and archiving domain with numerous capabilities such as action CRUD, deed entry lifecycle management, document metadata handling, key management, archiving, reporting, and scheduled jobs, all secured by Spring Security. While the codebase demonstrates moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion, there are architectural debts including oversized controllers, missing service interfaces, misplaced DAO implementations, and a lack of an application façade to isolate the domain, resulting in an overall quality grade of B.

capabilities: Create, read, update, and delete actions
Manage deed entries and their lifecycle
Store and retrieve document metadata
Handle handover data sets and history
Perform key management operations (reencryption, crypto state)
Execute archiving processes and sign submission/re‑encryption tokens
Provide business purpose lookup
Generate and serve reports
Schedule and run background jobs
Health checking and system monitoring
Secure REST API access with Spring Security
integrations: Relational database via Spring Data JPA/Hibernate
External Key Management System (via XnpKmServiceImpl / MockKmService)

debt_indicators: Large number of service implementations (42) without clear interfaces
DAO implementation classes placed in SERVICE layer
Potential tight coupling between controllers and repositories
Missing façade or application layer to isolate domain
recommendations: Introduce service interfaces and program to abstractions to decouple implementations
Extract common business logic into dedicated domain services or facades
Refactor oversized controllers into smaller, purpose‑driven components
Move DAO implementation classes out of the SERVICE package to respect layering
Consider adding an application façade layer (or hexagonal ports) to isolate external integrations
Review entity model to consolidate overlapping entities and reduce unnecessary complexity

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The backend provides solid functional coverage and leverages a familiar Spring stack, but architectural debt around layering violations and oversized components hampers maintainability and scalability. Addressing these issues will elevate the quality from a solid B to a higher tier.
top_recommendations: Introduce service interfaces and program to abstractions to decouple implementations
Refactor oversized controllers into smaller, purpose‑driven components
Add an application façade layer (or hexagonal ports) to isolate the domain from external integrations

container_analyses.summary: The frontend is a component‑based Angular application leveraging TypeScript and RxJS, organized into presentation, business‑logic, and data‑access layers and consuming a suite of REST APIs. It fully supports the legal deed management domain with capabilities ranging from deed creation to PDF generation and background job handling. While the codebase exhibits high cohesion, moderate coupling and several areas of technical debt—especially an excess of services, adapters, and pipes—its overall quality is solid (grade B). Targeted refactoring and consolidation will improve maintainability and reduce complexity.

layers: Data Access / Integration (Adapters, Interceptors, API Modules)

functional.bounded_context: Legal Deed Management and Document Lifecycle
capabilities: Create, edit and view deeds
Approve and correct deed entries
Manage deed lifecycle workflows
Handle document import/export and PDF generation
Capture and validate electronic signatures
Perform user authentication and authorization
Apply security features such as reencryption
Generate and view reports and metadata
Execute background jobs and task processing
Provide online help and contextual assistance

debt_indicators: Excessive number of services (131) suggesting possible god services
Large number of adapters (50) with similar naming, indicating duplicated abstractions
Proliferation of pipes (67) that may be split across unrelated concerns
Modules contain many responsibilities; some modules may be too broad
recommendations: Consolidate related services into domain‑specific service groups or facades to reduce coupling
Introduce higher‑level abstractions (e.g., repository or gateway patterns) to unify similar adapters
Review and refactor rarely‑used or overly granular pipes; group them into shared utility libraries
Apply the Single Responsibility Principle to modules: split overly large modules into feature‑specific ones
Add interface contracts for critical services to decouple implementations from consumers

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The frontend delivers comprehensive functional coverage and adheres to a solid component‑based architecture, but moderate coupling and notable technical debt (especially service and adapter sprawl) warrant focused refactoring to sustain long‑term quality.
top_recommendations: Consolidate related services into domain‑specific service groups or facades
Introduce repository/gateway abstractions to unify and deduplicate adapters
Refactor oversized modules and split them into feature‑specific modules

layers: Data Access / Integration (Adapters, Interceptors, API Modules)

executive_summary: Map-Reduce analysis completed for 4 containers with 737 components.
top_recommendations: Consider extracting large containers into separate services
Add API documentation for external interfaces
Implement health checks for all containers
